quiz.title: Unit 9 Quiz 10, Biology I
quiz.subtitle: Genetics: Dihybrid Crosses and Gamete Formation
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Dihybrid Crosses, True-Breeding, Gamete Types, Phenotypic Ratios, Parental Genotypes

questions.question: In peas, inflated pods (I) are dominant over constricted pods (i). If a pure inflated pod plant is crossed with a constricted pod plant, what is the ratio of heterozygous inflated (Ii) to pure constricted (ii) plants in F₂?
questions.explanation: Pure inflated (II) × constricted (ii) produces all Ii in F₁. F₁ self-cross (Ii × Ii) gives genotypic ratio 1 II : 2 Ii : 1 ii in F₂. Heterozygous inflated (Ii) to pure constricted (ii) ratio = 2 : 1.

questions.question: A pure round seed plant (RR) is crossed with a wrinkled seed plant (rr). The F₂ generation is produced by self-pollinating the F₁. What is the ratio of pure round (RR) to hybrid round (Rr) plants?
questions.explanation: RR × rr produces all Rr in F₁. F₁ self-cross (Rr × Rr) gives genotypic ratio 1 RR : 2 Rr : 1 rr in F₂. Pure round (RR) to hybrid round (Rr) ratio = 1 : 2.

questions.question: A pure purple flower plant (PP) is crossed with a white flower plant (pp). In the F₂ generation, what is the ratio of total purple-flowered plants to pure white-flowered plants?
questions.explanation: PP × pp produces all Pp in F₁. F₁ self-cross (Pp × Pp) gives phenotypic ratio 3 purple (PP and Pp) : 1 white (pp) in F₂. Ratio of total purple to pure white = 3 : 1.

questions.question: In a cross between two pea plants, 100% of the F₁ generation are Tall and Purple. If one parent was dwarf and white (ttpp), what was the genotype of the other parent?
questions.explanation: One parent is ttpp (dwarf, white). For all F₁ to be tall and purple, the other parent must contribute dominant alleles for both traits. The only genotype that ensures all offspring get T and P alleles when crossed with ttpp is TTPP (homozygous dominant for both traits).

questions.question: What is the probability of getting a recessive phenotype for all three gene locus for the cross Aa Bb Cc × aa Bb cc?
questions.explanation:
Calculate each gene separately:

1. A locus: Aa × aa → probability of aa (recessive phenotype) = 1/2
2. B locus: Bb × Bb → probability of bb (recessive phenotype) = 1/4
3. C locus: Cc × cc → probability of cc (recessive phenotype) = 1/2

Multiply independent probabilities: 1/2 × 1/4 × 1/2 = 1/16

questions.question: Two black female mice are coupled with a brown male mouse. If female A produces 4 black and 3 brown children and female B in its litter produces 10 black children, what is the genotype of A, B and male mouse respectively?
questions.explanation: Brown male is recessive (bb) because brown is recessive to black. Female A produces both black and brown offspring (approximately 1:1 ratio) → must be heterozygous (Bb) crossed with bb. Female B produces only black offspring (all Bb) → must be homozygous dominant (BB) crossed with bb. So A = Bb, B = BB, male = bb. Option C matches: Bb, BB and bb.

questions.question: Which of the following harmful mutations will have the most severe effect in the progeny?
questions.explanation: Mutations in gametes (sperm or egg cells) can be passed to offspring and affect all cells of the next generation. Mutations in somatic cells (muscle, mammary gland, sperm mother cells before they become gametes) are not heritable and affect only the individual. Mutation in a gamete has the most severe effect on progeny because it can be inherited.

questions.question: You observe a genetic trait which is a common occurrence in the male of a particular pedigree, this is possibly a ______
questions.explanation: If a trait occurs commonly in males and is passed from father to son, it is likely Y-linked (holandric) inheritance. Y-linked traits appear only in males and are passed from fathers to all sons. X-linked recessive traits affect males more frequently than females, but they are not exclusive to males and can skip generations. The phrasing 'common occurrence in the male' suggests Y-linked inheritance.

questions.question: If you cross a white eyed female drosophila with a red eyed male drosophila, what will be the colour of eyes for their male and female offspring?
options: Red eyed daughter and white eyed son
Red eyed son and white eyed daughter
questions.explanation: Eye color in Drosophila is X-linked. White eye is recessive (XʳXʳ female), red eye is dominant (XᴿY male). Cross: XʳXʳ × XᴿY. Females get Xʳ from mother and Xᴿ from father → XᴿXʳ (red-eyed, heterozygous). Males get Xʳ from mother and Y from father → XʳY (white-eyed). Result: red-eyed daughters, white-eyed sons.

questions.question: If a genotype is not known it is crossed with recessive parent to reveal what is hidden then the cross is known as:
questions.explanation: A test cross involves crossing an individual with an unknown genotype (but dominant phenotype) with a homozygous recessive individual. The offspring ratios reveal whether the unknown parent is homozygous dominant or heterozygous. This is specifically designed to 'test' or reveal the hidden genotype.

questions.question: Male to male transmission is a key feature of which pattern of inheritance?
questions.explanation: Male-to-male transmission (father to son) is a key feature of autosomal inheritance because fathers pass autosomes to both sons and daughters equally. X-linked traits cannot be passed from father to son because fathers give their X chromosome to daughters and Y chromosome to sons. Both autosomal dominant and recessive patterns can show male-to-male transmission, but among the options, autosomal dominant is correct. X-linked traits never show father-to-son transmission.

questions.question: A plant has the genotype Rryy (Round and Green). During meiosis, what are the possible types of gametes this plant can produce?
questions.explanation: Rryy has two genes: Rr (heterozygous) and yy (homozygous recessive). Possible gamete combinations: R with y, and r with y → Ry and ry. Gametes are haploid, so they cannot contain two alleles of same gene (like RR or yy).

questions.question: You cross two F₁ plants that are both heterozygous (RrYy). Out of 16 offspring in the F₂ generation, how many are expected to show both recessive traits (wrinkled and green)?
questions.explanation: RrYy × RrYy is a dihybrid cross. Probability of wrinkled (rr) = 1/4, green (yy) = 1/4. Combined probability = 1/4 × 1/4 = 1/16. Out of 16 offspring, 1 is expected to be wrinkled and green (rryy).

questions.question: If 'Y' represents Yellow seeds and 'y' represents Green seeds, and 'R' represents Round while 'r' represents Wrinkled, which of the following genotypes will result in a Green, Round seed?
questions.explanation: Green seeds require yy (homozygous recessive for color). Round seeds require at least one R (R_ ). yyRr fits: green (yy) and round (Rr). YYRR and YyRr are yellow, yyrr is green but wrinkled.

questions.question: A scientist wants to perform a dihybrid cross. She selects two 'True-Breeding' parents: one with Inflated, Green pods and one with Constricted, Yellow pods. What will the F₁ generation look like?
questions.explanation: True-breeding Inflated, Green = homozygous for both traits. Inflated (I) dominant, Green (g) recessive → genotype: IIgg. Constricted, Yellow = constricted (i) recessive, Yellow (G) dominant → genotype: iiGG. IIgg × iiGG produces all IiGg in F₁. IiGg expresses dominant traits: Inflated (I) and Yellow (G). All F₁ are Inflated-Yellow.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered dihybrid crosses and gamete formation! 🧬
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review genotypic ratios and true-breeding parent combinations.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on gamete possibilities and phenotypic outcomes.
scoreMessages.0: Please review dihybrid cross probabilities, gamete formation, and dominant/recessive relationships.
